topic.title: Uber: SWE Internship 24-25| 18th October 2024 [6 months] OA.

post.content:
This OA was for 2025 6 month internship programm for freshers of 2025!
Total 3 questions for a duration of 90 minutes
Distribution marks were 1st) 100  2nd) 200  3rd) 300. 
I could not solve even the  1st question, let alone 2nd and 3rd.

Is this level of DSA skills are needed to be hired these days??

![image](https://assets.leetcode.com/users/images/2fd1b836-19f1-461f-865d-1fc1ac392b3f_1729260157.7773848.png)
[image](https://assets.leetcode.com/users/images/216d14c2-f58b-4099-884e-a29f3b85ef46_1729258694.8828957.png)
![image](https://assets.leetcode.com/users/images/a4f5359d-249e-4d98-b42f-c31f6f6de8a0_1729258694.9563124.png)
![image](https://assets.leetcode.com/users/images/3b392ac4-741a-4877-9df6-ba9eb8f1a7c7_1729258695.039427.png)
![image](https://assets.leetcode.com/users/images/c7f34b87-8398-48bc-8fec-bf17978d4028_1729258695.0071383.png)

**Q1: remainder of giant number:** remainder is mod r, and `p**q! % r == (p%r)**q!`

Anon's number theory answer is probably right, but I can't follow it because it's too smart for me. And we probably have to worry about `p, q!, r` being coprime or not.

Brute force approach: `p**(q!) % r == (p%r)**(q!) % r == (p%r)**1**2**3**4...**q % r`. With the classic binary scaling-and-squaring algo each power mod `r` can be done in `log(power)` time. The max power is `q` so TC `O(q*log(q))` time. Constant space!

Fancier approach, TC `O(q+r)` and SC `O(r)`: since we're doing powers, let `a == p%r` and let's look at `a**1, a**2, a**3, a**4, ...` mod `r`. There are only `r` distinct residues mod `r`, `0..r-1`. So eventually we'll reach a cycle.

In `O(r)` we can find the prefix length and cycle length
* start with `a**1 % r` and record its power, `1`
* then do `a**(k-1) * k` and record `k` until we get back to a power we've already seen
* the prefix length is `k` and the cycle length is `k` minus the first earlier power we saw `a**k mod r` at
* record the head of the cycle, `a**k`

Then we know that `a**cycleLength == 1` and `a**(cycleLength*cycles) == 1**cycles == 1`.
* so get `remainder == q!-prefixLength % cycleLength` which we can do in `O(q)` time; get `q! % cycleLength` and subtract `prefixLength`
* then take the head of the cycle and multiply by `a**remainder` which is doable in `O(r)` time brute force because `remainder` is `O(r)`
* then we get an answer in `O(q+r)` time

I assume Anon's suggestion is basically the same thing. If `a` and `r` are relatively prime then `a**phi(r) == 1` so you can compute `phi(r)` and you can take `q! mod phi(r)` to get `remainder.` But if they're not relatively prime then life is hard and we need more number theory... or we can do the `O(r)` loop above!

If I had an hour for this in an OA I *might* do it, but the `O(q log(q))` solution is feasible in TLE and so much simpler that I'd just do that instead. Don't forget that OAs are partly graded on completion time and wrong submissions!

Some of the possible solutions.

Q1:

compute the euler totient function for r. This will allow you to use Euler theorem to reduce q! to some number in mod phi(r), which is less than r. You can do this in O(q + r) (can be made more efficient but not req)
Now that we have we have reduced our power from q! to a number less than r (say x times), simply multiply p x times while taking mod r.
Compl: O(p + q + r)

Q2:
This is one is just bruteforcing each substr combination in r. There are n^2 many r substrings and each comparison will take O(n^2). So total tc is O(n^4). To get the final ans you will just multiply occ(substr1, p) and occ(r-substr1, q) mod given.


Q3:
This can be solved greedily. find the element(p) with minimum a[p] value and do the same r[q].
Now for the rest of elements(say x) which are not p and q, you will add min(r[x]a[p],r[q]a[x]) to the total trip.
If p and q are different, you will also add a[p]*r[q]
if we try to find occurences of substring r in p+q wont it work?\n
What was your timming slot my was 3-5
The test was really tough! I was not able to solve any question completely, only some of the test cases were passed. What to expect next??
I also attended the test and was able to solve the first question with 70/100 marks and for the 3rd one, I was not able to pass the hidden test cases.

Didn't give the OA but these are the idea (I think, if anyone has them in an OJ can give code as well :p)

Q1: 
1. compute the [euler totient](https://cp-algorithms.com/algebra/phi-function.html)  function for r. This will allow you to use [Euler theorem](https://cp-algorithms.com/algebra/phi-function.html) to reduce q! to some number in mod phi(r), which is less than r. You can do this in O(q + r) (can be made more efficient but not req)
2. Now that we have we have reduced our power from q! to a number less than r (say x times), simply multiply p  x times while taking mod r.

Compl: O(p + q + r)

Q2:
This is one is just  bruteforcing each substr combination in r. There are n^2 many r substrings and each comparison will take O(n^2). So total tc is O(n^4). To get the final ans you will just multiply occ(substr1, p) and occ(r-substr1, q) mod given. 

Q3:
This can be solved greedily. find the element(p) with minimum a[p] value and do the same r[q]. 
Now for the rest of elements(say x) which are not p and q, you will add min(r[x]*a[p],r[q]*a[x]) to the total trip.
If p and q are different, you will also add a[p]*r[q]
